content: Tuberculosis kills 1.3 million people annually despite being completely curable with existing drugs and diagnostics. This is not a technical failure but a moral one — we have the tools and lack the will. The persistence of preventable diseases reveals that our global health system is designed around market logic rather than human need.
original_quote: Tuberculosis kills 1.3 million people a year, and we could stop it if we chose to. We have the drugs. We have the diagnostics. We lack the will.
implication: Leaders must recognize that most systemic problems are not about capability gaps but priority gaps. The question shifts from 'how do we solve this?' to 'why haven't we chosen to solve this?'

content: The fundamental question in global health is not technical capacity but moral boundaries: 'Who counts as we?' When we say 'we could end tuberculosis,' the pronoun reveals everything about inclusion, exclusion, and whose suffering registers as urgent versus abstract.
original_quote: The question is always: who counts as 'we'?
implication: Organizations claiming to solve global problems must first audit their definition of 'we' — whose voices are centered, whose needs are prioritized, and whose agency is recognized in solution design.

content: Global health failures persist because comfortable people do not care enough about uncomfortable people. The deadliest force in human history is not malice but indifference — the moral shrug that allows preventable suffering to continue because it happens to other people in other places.
implication: Effective advocacy requires making distant suffering proximate and personal. The challenge is not convincing people that problems exist, but making those problems feel urgent to people with power to act.
source_ref: Documented positions on global health equity

content: Everything is tuberculosis because every global health crisis reveals the same pattern: we have solutions that work for people who can afford them, and systems that abandon people who cannot. The disease becomes a lens for examining how wealth determines access to life itself.
implication: When designing solutions for global challenges, test them first on the most marginalized populations. If your solution doesn't work for people with the least power and resources, it's not actually a solution.

content: The moral architecture of global health is revealed in what we choose to fund versus what we choose to ignore. Diseases that affect wealthy populations attract research dollars and media attention, while diseases of poverty remain invisible despite affecting millions.
implication: Leaders must audit their resource allocation decisions for implicit bias. What problems get attention and investment reveals more about institutional values than any mission statement.
source_ref: Documented critiques of global health funding patterns

content: Treating global health as charity rather than justice fundamentally misframes the problem. Charity implies optional generosity toward the unfortunate; justice demands addressing systems that create and maintain health inequity as a structural feature.
implication: Organizations working on global issues must choose between a charity model (voluntary, feel-good, preserves status quo) and a justice model (mandatory, system-changing, redistributive). The framing determines both strategy and outcomes.
source_ref: Documented positions on global health framing

content: The geography of suffering is not random — it follows the geography of power. Diseases flourish where people lack political voice, economic leverage, and social capital. Public health is ultimately about power: who has it, who doesn't, and how it determines who lives and dies.
implication: Sustainable health interventions must address power imbalances, not just medical symptoms. Building local capacity and political agency is as important as delivering treatments.
source_ref: Documented analysis of health and power dynamics

content: We tell ourselves that global health problems are too complex to solve, but complexity is often a convenient excuse for inaction. Many interventions that work are relatively simple — the complexity lies in overcoming political and economic interests that benefit from the status quo.
implication: When facing seemingly intractable problems, separate technical complexity from political complexity. Often what looks like an unsolvable puzzle is actually a solvable problem with powerful stakeholders invested in non-solutions.
source_ref: Documented positions on global health complexity

content: The tuberculosis epidemic reveals how diseases become proxies for larger moral questions about human worth. Some lives are treated as more valuable, more worthy of investment, more deserving of attention — and these judgments become embedded in health systems as operational reality.
implication: Organizations must examine how their systems implicitly assign different values to different lives. Equal treatment requires actively countering societal biases, not just avoiding explicit discrimination.

content: Global health nationalism — prioritizing the health of your own citizens over others — seems natural but is ultimately self-defeating in an interconnected world. Diseases don't respect borders, and global health security requires global health equity.
implication: Leaders must make the case that local and global welfare are interdependent, not competing interests. Effective advocacy connects global solidarity to immediate self-interest.
source_ref: Documented positions on global health cooperation

content: The most dangerous myth in global health is that progress is inevitable — that technology and economic growth will naturally reduce suffering over time. Progress is a choice that requires sustained moral commitment, not an automatic byproduct of development.
implication: Sustainable change requires building institutions and cultures that actively choose equity, generation after generation. Progress is maintained through intentional effort, not maintained automatically.
source_ref: Documented critique of inevitable progress narratives

content: Pharmaceutical companies spend more money marketing drugs to rich populations than developing drugs for poor ones. This reveals how market logic — maximizing return on investment — becomes a death sentence for people whose lives aren't profitable enough to save.
implication: When market incentives conflict with human need, leaders must choose different frameworks for resource allocation. Some problems require non-market solutions because markets systematically undervalue certain lives.
source_ref: Documented critique of pharmaceutical industry priorities

content: Global health interventions often fail because they're designed by people who will never experience the problems they're trying to solve. Distance from suffering creates solutions that are technically sound but practically unusable by the people who need them most.
implication: Include affected communities as designers, not just beneficiaries, of solutions. The people closest to problems are often closest to solutions — but they need resources and decision-making power, not just consultation.
source_ref: Documented positions on community-centered health approaches

content: The language we use for global health — 'burden of disease,' 'target populations,' 'interventions' — reveals a clinical distance that enables moral distance. This technical vocabulary makes it easier to discuss millions of deaths as abstractions rather than tragedies.
implication: Audit your organization's language for dehumanizing patterns. Words that make suffering abstract enable inaction. Keeping human stories and faces central to conversations about systems can maintain moral urgency.
source_ref: Documented critique of global health terminology

content: The tuberculosis crisis persists because it primarily affects people who are already marginalized — the poor, the incarcerated, the undocumented. Diseases become neglected when they afflict populations that society has already chosen to neglect.
implication: Health equity requires addressing broader social justice issues. You cannot solve health problems for marginalized populations while leaving the systems that marginalize them intact.

content: Hope in global health cannot be passive optimism that things will improve. It must be the disciplined choice to act as if your actions matter, even when the problems seem impossibly large and the progress impossibly slow.
original_quote: Hope is not the belief that things will get better. It is the decision to act as if your actions matter, even when the evidence is ambiguous.
implication: Sustainable activism requires treating hope as a practice, not a feeling. Build systems that maintain commitment to long-term change even when short-term results are discouraging.
source_ref: Core philosophy on hope as discipline

content: The most radical act in global health is paying sustained attention to problems that the world has chosen to ignore. Attention is a form of moral action — what you choose to notice and keep noticing shapes what becomes possible to change.
original_quote: Attention is the most fundamental human act. What you choose to notice defines who you are.
implication: Leaders must treat attention as a strategic resource. Consistently highlighting neglected problems, keeping them visible in public discourse, creates the conditions for eventual action.
source_ref: Core philosophy on attention as moral practice
